From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British popular music of the late 1970s and early 1980s

Not to be confused with [Reggae punk](/wiki/Reggae_punk "Reggae punk").

For the record label, see [2 Tone Records](/wiki/2_Tone_Records "2 Tone Records"). For other uses, see [Two-tone (disambiguation)](/wiki/Two-tone_(disambiguation) "Two-tone (disambiguation)").

| Two-tone | |
| --- | --- |
| Other names | * 2 tone * ska-rock * ska revival |
| Stylistic origins | * [Ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") * [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") * [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") * [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") * [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music")[[1]](#cite_note-AllM_Revival-1) |
| Cultural origins | Late 1970s, [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), England |
| Derivative forms | * [Ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") * new tone[[2]](#cite_note-2) |
| Other topics | |
| * [2 Tone Records](/wiki/2_Tone_Records "2 Tone Records") * [reggae rock](/wiki/Reggae_rock "Reggae rock") | |

**Two-tone**, or **2 tone**, also known as **ska revival**,[[1]](#cite_note-AllM_Revival-1) is a genre of British popular music of the late 1970s and early 1980s that fused traditional [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), and [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") music with elements of [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") music.[[1]](#cite_note-AllM_Revival-1) Its name derives from [2 Tone Records](/wiki/2_Tone_Records "2 Tone Records"), a record label founded in 1979 by [Jerry Dammers](/wiki/Jerry_Dammers "Jerry Dammers") of [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and references a desire to transcend and defuse racial tensions in [Thatcher-era Britain](/wiki/Premiership_of_Margaret_Thatcher "Premiership of Margaret Thatcher"): many two-tone groups, such as [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials"), [the Selecter](/wiki/The_Selecter "The Selecter") and [the Beat](/wiki/The_Beat_(British_band) "The Beat (British band)"), featured a mix of [black](/wiki/Black_people "Black people"), [white](/wiki/White_people "White people"), and [multiracial people](/wiki/Multiracial_people "Multiracial people").

Originating in [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") in the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") of [England](/wiki/England "England") in the late 1970s, it was part of the second wave of ska music. It followed on from the first ska music that developed in Jamaica in the 1950s and 1960s, infused with [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music") textures.

Although two-tone's mainstream commercial appeal was largely limited to the UK, it influenced the [ska punk](/wiki/Ska_punk "Ska punk") movement that developed in the US in the late 1980s and 1990s.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Two-tone_(music_genre)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The two-tone sound originated among young musicians in [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") in the West Midlands of England, who grew up listening to 1960s [Jamaican music](/wiki/Jamaican_music "Jamaican music").[[6]](#cite_note-6) They combined influences from [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") and [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") with elements of [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music"). Bands considered part of the genre include [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials"), [the Selecter](/wiki/The_Selecter "The Selecter"), [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)"), [the Beat](/wiki/The_Beat_(British_band) "The Beat (British band)"), [Bad Manners](/wiki/Bad_Manners "Bad Manners"), [the Bodysnatchers](/wiki/The_Bodysnatchers_(band) "The Bodysnatchers (band)") and [Akrylykz](/wiki/Akrylykz "Akrylykz").

The Specials' keyboard player [Jerry Dammers](/wiki/Jerry_Dammers "Jerry Dammers") coined the term "two-tone". Dammers, with the assistance of [Horace Panter](/wiki/Horace_Panter "Horace Panter") and graphic designer John "Teflon" Sims, developed the iconic *Walt Jabsco* logo (a man in a black suit, white shirt, black tie, [pork pie hat](/wiki/Pork_pie_hat "Pork pie hat"), white socks and black [loafers](/wiki/Loafers "Loafers")) to represent the two-tone genre. The logo, based on an early album-cover photo of [Peter Tosh](/wiki/Peter_Tosh "Peter Tosh"), included an added black-and-white [check pattern](/wiki/Check_(pattern) "Check (pattern)").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Most of the bands considered to be part of the two-tone genre were signed to [2 Tone Records](/wiki/2_Tone_Records "2 Tone Records") (operative 1979–1985) at some point. Other record labels associated with the two-tone sound were [Stiff Records](/wiki/Stiff_Records "Stiff Records") and [Go Feet Records](/wiki/Go_Feet_Records "Go Feet Records"). The music was especially popular among [skinheads](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead"), [rudies](/wiki/Rude_Boy "Rude Boy") and [mod revivalists](/wiki/Mod_revival "Mod revival").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Museum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Two-tone_(music_genre)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Museum")]

On 1 October 2010, the 2-Tone Central museum, cafe and venue opened in the [Coventry University Students' Union](/wiki/Coventry_University_Students%27_Union "Coventry University Students' Union") building, and by August 2011 it had moved to the 2-Tone Village in [Stoke, Coventry](/wiki/Stoke,_Coventry "Stoke, Coventry").[[10]](#cite_note-10) It includes exhibition space and the Coventry Music Wall of Fame.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
